summary: Apple's iOS 17.3 beta introduces Stolen Device Protection, a new feature designed to prevent thieves who know a victim's passcode from taking over their entire digital life. The feature requires biometric authentication plus an hour-long delay for sensitive actions like changing an Apple ID password when away from familiar locations. Gruber traces the feature back to Joanna Stern and Nicole Nguyen's investigative reporting at the WSJ, which exposed how thieves were exploiting the god-like powers of device passcodes. He explains why Apple allowed passcode-based account resets in the first place: orders of magnitude more people forget their iCloud passwords than fall victim to thieves. While praising the feature's balance of security and convenience, Gruber questions how 'home' and 'work' safe locations are determined, noting his own test iPhone didn't recognize his home.
key_points: Stolen Device Protection uses a combination of biometrics and a one-hour time delay to prevent thieves from resetting Apple ID passwords, with exceptions for familiar locations like home and work
The feature exists because device passcodes currently grant god-like powers — including the ability to reset iCloud passwords, access saved passwords, and lock victims out of Find My
Apple allows passcode-based account resets because far more people forget their iCloud password than are victimized by thieves — Android works the same way
The feature will be off by default but users will be prompted about it after upgrading, which Gruber considers a reasonable compromise
Gruber's main concern is how familiar locations are determined, since his own test iPhone at home still triggered the hour-long delay
money_quotes: This sounds like a very thoughtful solution to a devilishly tricky problem: a combination of biometrics and a time delay, with exceptions for when you're in a known location like work or home.
The gist of it is that your device passcode/passphrase controls the keys to your entire digital kingdom.
With the phone and your passcode, you can reset your iCloud account password and access the passwords saved in your keychain.

However many people are falling victim to thieves taking advantage of this, there are orders of magnitude more innocent users who do know their phone passcode, but have forgotten their iCloud/Google account password.
Stolen Device Protection addresses the problem well, with balance between security and convenience.
No existing workaround is a true defense against a thief who knows your device passcode.
Overall, this new feature is clearly a win for security — and a triumph of Joanna Stern and Nicole Nguyen's investigative reporting.
key_insight: Stolen Device Protection elegantly balances the tension between protecting victims of phone theft and serving the far larger population of users who simply forget their passwords, though questions remain about how it determines trusted locations.
